comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the technical and artistic aspects of the dithering technique used in the game Return of the Obra Dinn. Commenters share resources, personal experiences with the game, and technical questions about the dithering process and its visual effects.

comments.author: skrebbel
comments.text:
This is so cool! Excellent writeup also.

I feel like I understand it all except the last step:

> I could feel the closeness, and a very simple fix for this kind of aliasing is to supersample: apply the dither thresholding at a higher resolution and downsample.

Here he shows a dither pattern that isn't monochrome, but has grays (cause it's downsampled). But the picture in the end is monochrome again. How does this work? How does he downsample the dithered result while staying monochrome?
comments.analysis: This comment shows a clear understanding of the technical content and asks a relevant, insightful question about the dithering process, indicating thoughtful engagement.

comments.author: abetusk
comments.text:
I keep feeling like there's a set of fundamental assumptions that can be optimized for, or relaxed and optimized for, in order to get at what a better method might be.

For example, stability of dithering under rotation and or some type of shear translation. What about stability under scaling?

There's been some other methods that essentially create a dither texture on the surface itself but, to me at least, this has a different quality than the "screen space" dithering that Obra Dinn employs.

Does anyone have any ideas on how to make this idea more rigorous? Or is the set of assumption fundamentally contradictory?
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully explores potential improvements and theoretical considerations about dithering stability and methods, contributing meaningfully to the discussion.

comments.author: QuaternionsBhop
comments.text: The final dithering effect appears to be mis-sampled and is no longer 1 bit per pixel
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out a technical detail about the dithering output not maintaining strict 1-bit per pixel, which aligns with other comments and technical explanations.

comments.author: tesseractcat
comments.text: If anyone finds this interesting, I'd like to plug my post analyzing a similar technique, but generalized for perspective pixel art: https://tesseractc.at/shadowglass
comments.analysis: This comment adds value by sharing a related resource and analysis, helping to broaden the discussion with relevant technical content.

comments.author: mblode
comments.text:
I built a mini game with this technique [1][2]

[1] https://dither.blode.co

[2] https://github.com/mblode/dither-3d
comments.analysis: The author contributes practical experience and resources, which supports the technical discussion and shows applied understanding.

comments.author: vagab0nd
comments.text:
This is great from a technical and artistic perspective. But for me personally, the visual style ruined a great game. I love detective/deduction games. I'm listing some of my all-time favorites in this genre. I'd love to finish Obra Dinn, but god it just makes my eyes hurt so much.

The Case of the Golden Idol

Chants of Sennaar

Her Story

IMMORTALITY

The Painscreek Killings

The Roottrees are Dead

Type Help
comments.analysis: This comment provides a personal, subjective perspective on the game's visual style, highlighting how technical artistry can affect user experience differently.

comments.author: antics
comments.text:
> It feels a little weird to put 100 hours into something that won't be noticed by its absence. Exactly no one will think, "man this dithering is stable as shit. total magic going on here." I don't want to give people problems they didn't know they should have though so it was worth fixing.

I mean maybe it's just me, but that is literally the first thing I noticed and I appreciated it so much I instantly bought the game. I don't even play video games much!
comments.analysis: This comment reflects emotional appreciation for the technical detail, showing how such subtle features can impact player engagement.

comments.author: progforlyfe
comments.text: I grew up on the small 6 inch 1 bit Mac SE display so the art style has a special place in my heart. Sadly I'm too "dumb" to fully enjoy the game as it requires a lot of attention to detail -- amazing if you enjoy detective style puzzles! I still highly respect it.
comments.analysis: This comment adds nostalgic context and personal connection to the art style, enriching the discussion with emotional depth.

comments.author: rokkamokka
comments.text: I gave this game a shot but honestly the art style got in the way of the gameplay for me. Fun to read how much effort went into it
comments.analysis: A straightforward personal critique highlighting that the visual style can detract from gameplay experience for some players.

comments.author: m12k
comments.text: If you find this interesting, you might also be interested in this video of someone diving even deeper into how to make the dither surface stable: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HPqGaIMVuLs
comments.analysis: This comment shares a relevant external resource, contributing constructively to the technical discussion.
